name: Apple
explanation: Apples are high in both fructose and sorbitol, making them one of the most common FODMAP triggers for people with IBS.
serving_note: No safe serving size established — even small amounts may trigger symptoms.

name: Apricot
explanation: Apricots are very high in sorbitol, a sugar alcohol that is poorly absorbed in the small intestine.
serving_note: No safe serving size — avoid entirely during the elimination phase.

name: Avocado
explanation: Avocado contains sorbitol, but only becomes high FODMAP above a small portion. A small amount is generally tolerated.
serving_note: Safe up to 30g (⅛ of an avocado). One third or more becomes high FODMAP.

name: Banana
explanation: Ripe bananas become higher in fructose as they ripen. Unripe (slightly green) bananas are lower in FODMAPs.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ medium banana (100g). A whole ripe banana may be too high for sensitive individuals.

name: Blueberries
explanation: Blueberries are Low FODMAP at typical serving sizes and are a great fruit option for people with IBS.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (75g). Larger portions may contain moderate amounts of fructose.

name: Cherry
explanation: Cherries are very high in both sorbitol and fructose and should be avoided on a Low FODMAP diet.
serving_note: No safe serving size — avoid entirely during elimination.

name: Grapefruit
explanation: Grapefruit is Low FODMAP and safe to eat at normal serving sizes.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ medium grapefruit. Enjoy freely within reasonable portions.

name: Kiwi
explanation: Kiwi is Low FODMAP and well tolerated by most people with IBS. It's also a natural digestive aid.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 medium kiwi fruits.

name: Mango
explanation: Mango is very high in fructose and is one of the highest FODMAP fruits. Even small servings can be problematic.
serving_note: No safe serving size during elimination. Small amounts (40g) may be tried during reintroduction.

name: Orange
explanation: Oranges are Low FODMAP and a great source of vitamin C. Safe to enjoy regularly.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 medium orange. Limit juice to 125ml (½ cup).

name: Papaya
explanation: Papaya (pawpaw) is Low FODMAP and also contains papain, an enzyme that may aid digestion.
serving_note: Safe up to 140g (about 1 cup cubed).

name: Peach
explanation: Peaches are high in both sorbitol and fructose, making them a significant FODMAP trigger.
serving_note: No safe serving size during elimination phase.

name: Pear
explanation: Pears are very high in fructose and sorbitol. They are one of the highest FODMAP fruits and are a well-known IBS trigger.
serving_note: No safe serving size — avoid entirely.

name: Pineapple
explanation: Pineapple is Low FODMAP and contains bromelain, an enzyme that may support digestion.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 cup (140g) of fresh pineapple.

name: Raspberries
explanation: Raspberries are Low FODMAP at typical serving sizes and packed with antioxidants.
serving_note: Safe up to ⅓ cup (40g). Larger portions may become moderate.

name: Strawberries
explanation: Strawberries are Low FODMAP and one of the best fruit options for people following a Low FODMAP diet.
serving_note: Safe up to 10 medium berries (150g).

name: Watermelon
explanation: Watermelon is very high in fructose and mannitol. Even small servings are problematic for most people with IBS.
serving_note: No safe serving size during elimination. Avoid entirely.

name: Garlic
explanation: Garlic is extremely high in fructans and is one of the most potent FODMAP triggers. Even small amounts in cooked dishes can cause symptoms.
serving_note: No safe serving size — avoid all forms including powder and in cooked dishes. FODMAPs from garlic do leach into oil during cooking.

name: Garlic-Infused Oil
explanation: Garlic-infused oil is safe because fructans (the problematic FODMAPs in garlic) are water-soluble, not fat-soluble. They don't transfer into oil.
serving_note: Safe to use freely — it provides garlic flavour without the FODMAPs.

name: Onion
explanation: Onion is one of the highest FODMAP vegetables, extremely high in fructans. It is a very common IBS trigger and should be avoided in all forms — raw, cooked, or powdered.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Even onion powder in very small amounts can trigger symptoms.

name: Spring Onion (Green Tops)
explanation: The green tops of spring onions (scallions) are Low FODMAP. The white bulb, however, is high in fructans and should be avoided.
serving_note: Use green tops only. Safe at ¼ cup. Avoid the white part entirely.

name: Leek
explanation: The white and light green parts of leeks are high in fructans. Only the dark green tops are low FODMAP.
serving_note: Avoid the bulb/white part. Dark green tops (½ cup) are safe as an alternative.

name: Carrot
explanation: Carrots are Low FODMAP and one of the safest vegetables for people with IBS. Enjoy freely.
serving_note: Safe at all typical serving sizes. No restriction needed.

name: Broccoli
explanation: Broccoli is moderate in FODMAPs. The florets are higher than the stalks. Small portions are generally safe.
serving_note: Safe up to ¾ cup (75g) florets. Larger servings become high FODMAP.

name: Cauliflower
explanation: Cauliflower is moderate in FODMAPs, containing both fructans and mannitol. It can be eaten in small portions.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (75g). A full cup or more is high FODMAP.

name: Celery
explanation: Celery is high in mannitol and becomes problematic even in small servings. It's best avoided during the elimination phase.
serving_note: No established safe serving size. Avoid during elimination.

name: Cucumber
explanation: Cucumber is Low FODMAP and a great addition to salads and snacks.
serving_note: Safe at all typical serving sizes.

name: Mushrooms
explanation: Most common mushrooms (button, cremini, portobello) are very high in mannitol. They are a frequent and often-overlooked FODMAP trigger.
serving_note: No safe serving size for common varieties. Oyster mushrooms are low FODMAP and a good alternative.

name: Spinach
explanation: Fresh baby spinach is Low FODMAP. It's a versatile and nutritious green that works well in most dishes.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 cup (75g) fresh. Baby spinach is preferred over mature leaves.

name: Tomato
explanation: Tomatoes are Low FODMAP at typical serving sizes. Cherry tomatoes, roma tomatoes, and common varieties are all safe.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 medium tomato or ½ cup cherry tomatoes.

name: Bell Pepper
explanation: Bell peppers (capsicum) are Low FODMAP. Red peppers tend to be slightly higher in fructose but still safe at typical servings.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ medium pepper (52g). Green bell peppers are the lowest FODMAP choice.

name: Sweet Potato
explanation: Sweet potato contains mannitol and becomes high FODMAP in larger servings. Small portions are generally tolerated.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (70g). A full cup or medium sweet potato is high FODMAP.

name: Potato
explanation: White potato is Low FODMAP and a safe starchy option. Prepare without garlic or onion-based seasonings.
serving_note: Safe at typical serving sizes. Enjoy boiled, baked, or roasted.

name: Zucchini
explanation: Zucchini (courgette) is Low FODMAP and very versatile. It works well raw, roasted, or spiralised as a pasta alternative.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (65g). Larger amounts may become moderate.

name: Eggplant
explanation: Eggplant (aubergine) is Low FODMAP at typical serving sizes and is a great meat-free option.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 cup (75g). Large amounts may have small amounts of fructans.

name: Asparagus
explanation: Asparagus is high in fructans, especially in the tips. It's a well-known digestive irritant for people with IBS.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Even 2–3 spears can trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.

name: Green Beans
explanation: Green beans (French beans, string beans) are Low FODMAP and a great vegetable choice.
serving_note: Safe up to 15 beans (75g). Very large servings become moderate.

name: Kale
explanation: Kale is Low FODMAP and highly nutritious. Suitable for salads, soups, and smoothies.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 cup (75g) chopped.

name: Corn
explanation: Corn contains fructans and sorbitol, making it moderate in FODMAPs. A small serving is usually tolerated.
serving_note: Safe at ½ cob or ½ cup kernels (38g). Larger servings become high FODMAP.

name: Peas
explanation: Regular green peas are high in both fructans and GOS, making them a significant FODMAP food to avoid.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Snow peas (2 pods) and sugar snap peas (5 pods) are lower FODMAP alternatives.

name: Cow's Milk
explanation: Regular cow's milk is high in lactose. Lactose is the primary FODMAP in dairy products and a very common IBS trigger.
serving_note: No safe serving size if lactose intolerant. Even 100ml can cause symptoms.

name: Lactose-Free Milk
explanation: Lactose-free milk has the same taste and nutrition as regular milk but with the lactose broken down. It's a perfect 1:1 swap.
serving_note: Safe at normal milk serving sizes. No restriction needed.

name: Almond Milk
explanation: Unsweetened almond milk is Low FODMAP and a great dairy alternative. Check labels to avoid added inulin or chicory root.
serving_note: Safe at 240ml (1 cup). Always choose unsweetened varieties.

name: Oat Milk
explanation: Oat milk can contain fructans from oats. The amount varies by brand. Small amounts are usually tolerated.
serving_note: Safe at 30ml (2 tbsp). A full glass (240ml) may be high FODMAP depending on the brand.

name: Cheddar Cheese
explanation: Aged hard cheeses like cheddar are very low in lactose because most of it is lost during the aging process. Safe for most people with lactose sensitivity.
serving_note: Safe at typical serving sizes (40g / 2 slices).

name: Parmesan
explanation: Parmesan is an aged hard cheese and is very low in lactose. Safe to use in cooking and as a topping.
serving_note: Safe at normal cooking amounts (40g).

name: Feta Cheese
explanation: Feta is a Low FODMAP cheese due to its low lactose content, making it a great addition to salads and Mediterranean dishes.
serving_note: Safe up to 40g per serving.

name: Yogurt (Regular)
explanation: Regular yogurt retains a significant amount of lactose and is high FODMAP for most people with IBS.
serving_note: Avoid regular yogurt. Choose lactose-free or plant-based alternatives.

name: Cream
explanation: Cream is high in lactose and a common trigger for people with IBS. Even small amounts in cooking can cause issues.
serving_note: Avoid in larger amounts. Replace with lactose-free cream or coconut cream.

name: Coconut Cream
explanation: Canned coconut cream is Low FODMAP and a great dairy-free alternative in cooking and baking.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (125ml). Full-fat canned coconut cream is preferred.

name: Butter
explanation: Butter contains only trace amounts of lactose and is considered Low FODMAP. Suitable for cooking and baking.
serving_note: Safe at normal cooking amounts (1–2 tbsp).

name: Wheat Bread
explanation: Regular wheat bread is high in fructans and is one of the most common FODMAP triggers. This includes white and wholegrain wheat bread.
serving_note: Avoid regular wheat bread during elimination. Choose gluten-free bread or sourdough alternatives.

name: Gluten-Free Bread
explanation: Most gluten-free breads are Low FODMAP as they avoid wheat-based fructans. Always check labels for added FODMAP ingredients like apple juice or honey.
serving_note: Safe at 2 slices. Check ingredients for inulin, chicory root, or fruit juices.

name: Sourdough Bread (Wheat)
explanation: Traditional long-fermented sourdough has lower fructan content because the fermentation process breaks down some FODMAPs. However, not all sourdough is made equally — fast-fermented versions may still be high.
serving_note: Safe at 1–2 slices of authentic, long-fermented sourdough. Commercial sourdough is often not truly fermented.

name: White Rice
explanation: White rice is one of the safest grains on a Low FODMAP diet. It's easily digestible and FODMAP-free.
serving_note: Safe at any normal serving size. A staple for the Low FODMAP diet.

name: Brown Rice
explanation: Brown rice is also Low FODMAP and provides additional fibre compared to white rice.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes (1 cup cooked).

name: Oats (Rolled)
explanation: Rolled oats contain some fructans but are Low FODMAP in small servings. They are a good breakfast option in moderation.
serving_note: Safe up to ½ cup (52g) dry oats. Larger portions become moderate to high FODMAP.

name: Quinoa
explanation: Quinoa is Low FODMAP and a great high-protein, gluten-free grain alternative.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 cup (155g) cooked.

name: Pasta (Wheat)
explanation: Regular wheat pasta is high in fructans. Cooking and rinsing reduces FODMAPs slightly but not enough for most people with IBS.
serving_note: Avoid during elimination. Choose gluten-free or rice-based pasta instead.

name: Pasta (Gluten-Free)
explanation: Gluten-free pasta (made from rice, corn, or quinoa) is Low FODMAP and is the go-to pasta substitute on this diet.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes (1 cup cooked). Check for wheat-free certification.

name: Chickpeas
explanation: Canned chickpeas that have been well-rinsed contain less FODMAPs than dried/home-cooked chickpeas because the FODMAPs leach into the canning liquid.
serving_note: Safe up to ¼ cup (42g) canned and well-rinsed. Larger portions are high FODMAP.

name: Lentils (Canned)
explanation: Canned and well-rinsed lentils are lower in FODMAPs than dried lentils. Small portions are manageable for many people.
serving_note: Safe up to ¼ cup (46g) canned, drained, and rinsed. Larger servings are high FODMAP.

name: Tofu (Firm)
explanation: Firm tofu is Low FODMAP because the FODMAPs in soybeans (GOS) are removed during the draining and pressing process. Silken tofu is NOT Low FODMAP.
serving_note: Safe up to 170g (¾ cup) of firm tofu. Avoid silken/soft tofu varieties.

name: Tempeh
explanation: Tempeh is Low FODMAP because the fermentation process breaks down the GOS in soybeans. It's a great protein source.
serving_note: Safe up to 100g per serving.

name: Black Beans
explanation: Black beans are high in GOS and fructans regardless of preparation method. They should be avoided during the elimination phase.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Choose tofu or tempeh as protein alternatives.

name: Chicken
explanation: Plain chicken is naturally Low FODMAP. Avoid marinades or seasonings that contain garlic, onion, or wheat.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes. Season with herbs, lemon, or garlic-infused oil.

name: Beef
explanation: Plain beef is naturally Low FODMAP. Avoid sauces or marinades containing FODMAP ingredients.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes. Watch out for pre-marinated or seasoned cuts.

name: Eggs
explanation: Eggs are completely Low FODMAP and are one of the best protein sources on this diet.
serving_note: Safe at any normal serving size. Versatile and easy to prepare.

name: Salmon
explanation: Salmon and most other fish are naturally Low FODMAP. Avoid fish with crumb coatings or sauces containing FODMAPs.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes. An excellent source of omega-3 fatty acids.

name: Shrimp / Prawns
explanation: Shrimp and prawns are Low FODMAP. Watch for added sauces or seasonings.
serving_note: Safe at normal serving sizes.

name: Coffee
explanation: Plain black coffee is Low FODMAP. However, it can still be a gut irritant for some people with IBS due to caffeine — not FODMAPs specifically.
serving_note: Safe up to 1–2 cups. Use lactose-free milk or a safe milk alternative if adding milk.

name: Green Tea
explanation: Green tea is Low FODMAP and a soothing beverage choice for people with IBS.
serving_note: Safe at 1–2 cups. Avoid adding honey (high FODMAP) — use a small amount of maple syrup if needed.

name: Apple Juice
explanation: Apple juice is very high in fructose and sorbitol. Fruit juices in general concentrate the FODMAPs from fruit and should be avoided.
serving_note: Avoid entirely. Choose water, orange juice (small glass), or herbal teas instead.

name: Orange Juice
explanation: A small glass of orange juice is Low FODMAP. Larger amounts may become moderate due to free fructose.
serving_note: Safe up to 125ml (½ cup). A full glass (250ml) becomes moderate FODMAP.

name: Beer
explanation: Regular beer is brewed from barley, which is high in fructans. It's a significant FODMAP source that should be avoided.
serving_note: Avoid regular beer. Gluten-free beer is generally Low FODMAP and a safer choice.

name: Wine (Dry)
explanation: Dry red or white wine is Low FODMAP at a standard serving size. Sweet wines may contain higher fructose levels.
serving_note: Safe up to 150ml (one standard glass) of dry wine. Alcohol itself can still irritate the gut — moderation is key.

name: Olive Oil
explanation: Olive oil is completely Low FODMAP. FODMAPs are water-soluble and do not exist in oils.
serving_note: Safe at all normal cooking amounts.

name: Soy Sauce
explanation: Regular soy sauce is Low FODMAP in small amounts. It is wheat-based but fermented, greatly reducing fructan content.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp. For a gluten-free option, use tamari.

name: Honey
explanation: Honey is very high in fructose, with a fructose-to-glucose ratio that makes it a high FODMAP sweetener.
serving_note: Avoid honey. Use maple syrup as a 1:1 substitute — it's Low FODMAP.

name: Maple Syrup
explanation: Pure maple syrup is Low FODMAP and a great substitute for honey or other high-fructose sweeteners.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp. Use pure maple syrup, not pancake syrup (often contains HFCS).

name: Mustard
explanation: Plain mustard (yellow, dijon) is Low FODMAP and safe to use as a condiment.
serving_note: Safe at typical condiment amounts. Avoid honey mustard (honey is high FODMAP).

name: Ketchup / Tomato Sauce
explanation: Ketchup may contain high-fructose corn syrup or concentrated fructose. Small amounts are usually tolerated but check labels carefully.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 tbsp. Larger amounts can become moderate FODMAP. Choose versions without HFCS.

name: Mayonnaise
explanation: Plain mayonnaise is Low FODMAP. It's made from eggs and oil, both of which are FODMAP-free.
serving_note: Safe at normal condiment amounts. Avoid flavoured varieties with garlic or onion.

name: Dark Chocolate
explanation: Dark chocolate contains some fructans from cocoa. Small amounts are Low FODMAP but a larger serve quickly becomes problematic.
serving_note: Safe up to 30g (about 5 squares) of 70%+ dark chocolate. Milk chocolate is higher FODMAP due to lactose.

name: Peanut Butter
explanation: Natural peanut butter (no added sweeteners) is Low FODMAP and a great source of protein and healthy fats.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp. Choose natural varieties without honey or high-fructose corn syrup.

name: Dates
explanation: Dates are extremely high in FODMAPs — they contain fructose, fructans, and sorbitol all at once. Even a single Medjool date contains enough FODMAPs to trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.
serving_note: No safe serving size during elimination. Avoid entirely, including date-based energy balls, bars, and natural sweeteners made from dates.

name: Hibiscus Tea
explanation: Dried hibiscus flowers are high in fructans, and these do leach into the water when brewed. A lightly steeped cup is often tolerated, but strong or long-brewed hibiscus tea can be a significant trigger.
serving_note: Limit to 1 cup, steeped no longer than 2–3 minutes. Avoid hibiscus-heavy blends or drinks made with hibiscus concentrate (e.g. agua de jamaica).

name: Peppermint Tea
explanation: Peppermint tea is Low FODMAP and actually one of the most recommended beverages for IBS — peppermint has antispasmodic properties that can help relieve gut discomfort.
serving_note: Safe at 1–2 cups. Avoid adding honey; use a small amount of maple syrup if needed.

name: Black Tea
explanation: Black tea is Low FODMAP. Like coffee, it can still stimulate the gut via caffeine, but it does not contain FODMAPs.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 cups. Use lactose-free milk or a safe plant milk if adding milk. Avoid adding honey.

name: Chamomile Tea
explanation: Chamomile flowers contain fructans that leach into hot water during steeping. A short steep is usually fine, but leaving the bag in too long or drinking multiple cups increases FODMAP load.
serving_note: Safe at 1 cup steeped for 1–2 minutes. Avoid concentrated chamomile teas or chamomile-based supplements.

name: Kombucha
explanation: Kombucha's FODMAP content varies widely by brand and batch — residual sugar (fructose) after fermentation is the main concern. Some bottles are fine; others have enough remaining fructose to trigger symptoms.
serving_note: If you tolerate it, limit to 180ml (¾ cup). Check labels — lower sugar content is generally safer. Homemade kombucha is harder to predict.

name: Garlic Powder
explanation: Garlic powder is far more concentrated than fresh garlic — a small pinch contains as many fructans as several fresh cloves. It's one of the most common hidden FODMAP triggers in packaged foods, spice blends, and restaurant cooking.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Avoid entirely, including in spice rubs, seasoning blends, and packaged soups or sauces. Always check ingredient labels for 'garlic powder' or 'garlic salt'.
alternative_names: Garlic-infused oil (fructans stay in the garlic, not the oil)

name: Onion Powder
explanation: Like garlic powder, onion powder is highly concentrated in fructans. It's a very common hidden ingredient in spice mixes, stock cubes, seasoning salts, and processed meats.
serving_note: No safe serving size. Check labels carefully — it appears on ingredients lists as 'onion powder', 'dried onion', 'onion salt', or 'onion flakes'.

name: BBQ Sauce
explanation: Most commercial BBQ sauces contain a double FODMAP hit: onion and garlic (fructans) plus high-fructose corn syrup or concentrated fruit juice (fructose). Even a small amount can be a significant trigger.
serving_note: Avoid standard BBQ sauces. Look for certified Low FODMAP brands, or make your own using garlic-infused oil, tomato paste, maple syrup, and smoked paprika.

name: Worcestershire Sauce
explanation: Worcestershire sauce contains onion and garlic, making it a FODMAP source. However, it's used in very small quantities and the concentration per serving is relatively low.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 tsp (5ml). Larger amounts — or using it as a marinade base — can accumulate fructans quickly.

name: Sriracha
explanation: Sriracha contains garlic as a primary ingredient — it's listed second on the label. This makes it high FODMAP even at small amounts.
serving_note: Avoid sriracha. Use plain chilli sauce or sambal oelek (check the label — some are garlic-free) for heat without the fructans.

name: Balsamic Vinegar
explanation: Balsamic vinegar is made from concentrated grape juice, which means it contains significant fructose. Small amounts used as a dressing are usually tolerated, but it's easy to over-pour.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 tbsp (15ml). Use a drizzle, not a generous pour. White wine vinegar or rice wine vinegar are safer alternatives for dressings.

name: Miso Paste
explanation: Miso is a fermented soybean paste. Fermentation reduces FODMAP content compared to plain soybeans, but at larger amounts the fructans become significant. Small amounts in soups or dressings are usually fine.
serving_note: Safe up to 1 tbsp (17g). Avoid drinking full bowls of miso soup made with extra paste, or using miso as a bulk marinade.

name: Tahini
explanation: Tahini (sesame seed paste) is Low FODMAP at normal serving sizes. Sesame seeds are FODMAP-free, making tahini a safe and nutritious condiment.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp (30g). Pair with lemon juice and garlic-infused oil for a Low FODMAP hummus-style dip.

name: Coconut Aminos
explanation: Coconut aminos is a soy-free, gluten-free alternative to soy sauce made from coconut sap. It's Low FODMAP and a great option for people avoiding both gluten and soy.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp. Slightly sweeter than soy sauce — adjust seasoning accordingly.

name: Red Onion
explanation: Red onion is high FODMAP, just like white and brown onion. It contains both fructans and excess fructose and is one of the most common IBS triggers — even small amounts cooked into a dish can cause symptoms.
serving_note: No safe serving size during elimination. This includes raw red onion in salads, pickled red onion, and red onion cooked into sauces or soups. The green tops of spring onions are a safe substitute.

name: Pesto
explanation: Traditional basil pesto contains garlic as a core ingredient, making it high FODMAP. Most store-bought pestos also use garlic powder, which is even more concentrated. The basil, pine nuts, and parmesan in pesto are all Low FODMAP — garlic is the only issue.
serving_note: Avoid standard pesto. Make your own using garlic-infused oil instead of garlic cloves — blend basil, garlic-infused oil, pine nuts, and parmesan for a fully Low FODMAP version. Some certified Low FODMAP pestos are available.

name: Cream Cheese
explanation: Cream cheese is a fresh soft cheese with a higher lactose content than aged cheeses. It becomes high FODMAP at larger amounts, but a small smear on toast or in a recipe is usually tolerated.
serving_note: Safe up to 2 tbsp (40g). Larger amounts — like using cream cheese as a filling or dip base — quickly push into high FODMAP territory. Lactose-free cream cheese is a safe alternative at any amount.
alternative_names: Lactose-free Greek yogurt (as a dip base)

name: Ricotta
explanation: Ricotta is a fresh whey cheese with high lactose content. Unlike aged cheeses where lactose is broken down during aging, ricotta is unaged and retains most of its lactose. It's a common trigger for people with IBS, especially when used in large portions like in lasagne or cheesecake.
serving_note: No safe serving size for most people with lactose sensitivity. Use lactose-free ricotta (widely available) or firm tofu blended with lemon juice as a ricotta substitute in cooked dishes.
alternative_names: Firm tofu (blended, as a cooked substitute)

name: Brie & Camembert
explanation: Brie and camembert are soft-ripened cheeses, but despite being fresh-looking they are actually Low FODMAP. The white mould ripening process breaks down most of the lactose, leaving very little by the time you eat it — similar to aged hard cheeses in this regard.
serving_note: Safe up to 40g per serving. This is one of the more surprising Low FODMAP foods — most people assume all soft cheeses are high FODMAP, but brie and camembert are exceptions.

name: Cottage Cheese
explanation: Cottage cheese is a fresh unaged cheese with moderate lactose. Small portions are often tolerated, but it's easy to overeat and push into high FODMAP territory.
serving_note: Safe up to 4 tbsp (120g). Choose lactose-free cottage cheese if you're particularly sensitive to lactose.
